*Os brancoazuis* (The Blue and Whites) *[Herculinos](/wiki/Tower_of_Hercules "Tower of Hercules")* (The Herculeans)[[1]](#cite_note-1)
8 December 1906; 119 years ago (1906-12-08)   as *Club Deportivo de la Sala Calvet*

infobox.key: Real Club Deportivo de La Coruña
infobox.value: (lit. 'Royal Sporting Club of La Coruña'), commonly known as **Deportivo La Coruña** (pronounced [[depoɾˈtiβo la koˈɾuɲa]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Spanish "Help:IPA/Spanish")) or simply **Depor**, is a Spanish professional [football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") club based in the city of [A Coruña](/wiki/A_Coru%C3%B1a "A Coruña"), Galicia, that competes in the [Segunda División](/wiki/Segunda_Divisi%C3%B3n "Segunda División"), the second tier of [Spanish football league system](/wiki/Spanish_football_league_system "Spanish football league system").

**Real Club Deportivo de La Coruña** (lit. 'Royal Sporting Club of La Coruña'), commonly known as **Deportivo La Coruña** (pronounced [[depoɾˈtiβo la koˈɾuɲa]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Spanish "Help:IPA/Spanish")) or simply **Depor**, is a Spanish professional [football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") club based in the city of [A Coruña](/wiki/A_Coru%C3%B1a "A Coruña"), Galicia, that competes in the [Segunda División](/wiki/Segunda_Divisi%C3%B3n "Segunda División"), the second tier of [Spanish football league system](/wiki/Spanish_football_league_system "Spanish football league system").

Founded in 1906 as *Club Deportivo de la Sala Calvet* by Federico Fernández-Amor Calvet, Deportivo have won the [La Liga](/wiki/La_Liga "La Liga") title once, in the [1999–2000 season](/wiki/1999%E2%80%932000_La_Liga "1999–2000 La Liga"), and finished as runner-up on five occasions.[[3]](#cite_note-:0-3) The club have also won the [Spanish Cup](/wiki/Copa_del_Rey "Copa del Rey") twice ([1994–95](/wiki/1994%E2%80%9395_Copa_del_Rey "1994–95 Copa del Rey") and [2001–02](/wiki/2001%E2%80%9302_Copa_del_Rey "2001–02 Copa del Rey")), three [Spanish Super Cups](/wiki/Supercopa_de_Espa%C3%B1a "Supercopa de España") (1995, 2000 and 2002), and the now defunct [Concurso España](/wiki/Concurso_Espa%C3%B1a "Concurso España") (1912). The *Blue and Whites* were a regular fixture in the top positions of La Liga for the best part of 20 years, from 1992 to 2010, finishing in the top half of the table in 16 out of 19 seasons, and are ranked 12th in the all-time La Liga table.[[4]](#cite_note-4) As a result, the club was a regular participant in European competitions, playing in the [UEFA Champions League](/wiki/UEFA_Champions_League "UEFA Champions League") for five consecutive seasons between 2000–01 and 2004–05, reaching the quarter-finals twice and reaching the semi-finals in the [2003–04](/wiki/2003%E2%80%9304_UEFA_Champions_League "2003–04 UEFA Champions League") season.

Deportivo have played their home games at the 32,490-capacity [Riazor](/wiki/Estadio_Riazor "Estadio Riazor") since 1944,[[5]](#cite_note-5) when the stadium was built. Their traditional home kit consists of blue and white striped shirts with blue shorts and socks. The club has a long-standing rivalry with [Celta Vigo](/wiki/Celta_Vigo "Celta Vigo"), and matches between the two sides are known as the [Galician derby](/wiki/Galician_derby "Galician derby").

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Deportivo_de_La_Coru%C3%B1a&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

Main article: [History of Deportivo La Coruña](/wiki/History_of_Deportivo_La_Coru%C3%B1a "History of Deportivo La Coruña")

### Foundation and first steps

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Deportivo_de_La_Coru%C3%B1a&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Foundation and first steps")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c9/Deportivo_da_Sala_Calvet_en_1908.jpg/250px-Deportivo_da_Sala_Calvet_en_1908.jpg)](/wiki/File:Deportivo_da_Sala_Calvet_en_1908.jpg)

Deportivo Sala Calvet in 1908

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b0/Deportivo_en_1912.png/250px-Deportivo_en_1912.png)](/wiki/File:Deportivo_en_1912.png)

Real Club Deportivo's first team in the 1912 [Concurso España](/wiki/Concurso_Espa%C3%B1a "Concurso España")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0f/Depor.PNG/250px-Depor.PNG)](/wiki/File:Depor.PNG)

Real Club Deportivo de La Coruña in 1927

Unlike what happened in most of the [Iberian Peninsula](/wiki/Iberian_Peninsula "Iberian Peninsula"), football did not reach the city of [A Coruña](/wiki/A_Coru%C3%B1a "A Coruña") through the English, instead, it was introduced by José María Ábalo, a youngster who had returned to his hometown after studying in England. The game gained rapid popularity and several teams were formed on an informal basis.

In December 1906, members of Sala Calvet gymnasium formed Deportivo de La Coruña, naming Luis Cornide as the first president.[[3]](#cite_note-:0-3) In May 1907, [Alfonso XIII of Spain](/wiki/Alfonso_XIII_of_Spain "Alfonso XIII of Spain") granted the club the *"Real"* ("royal") denomination. Dépor started playing at Corral de la Gaiteira ("Piper's Yard"), but soon moved to [Old Riazor](/wiki/Estadio_Riazor#History "Estadio Riazor"), a new ground near the Riazor beach.

The team played [friendly matches](/wiki/Exhibition_game#Associaiton_football "Exhibition game") regularly and competed in local leagues, but failed to achieve success in the [Copa del Rey](/wiki/Copa_del_Rey "Copa del Rey") in its early years. However, in 1912 Deportivo won their first official title, the short-lived [Concurso España](/wiki/Concurso_Espa%C3%B1a "Concurso España").[[6]](#cite_note-6)[[7]](#cite_note-Copa_España_oficial-7)[[8]](#cite_note-8)

In 1920, the [Spanish national team](/wiki/Spain_men%27s_national_football_team "Spain men's national football team") made its debut at the [Summer Olympics](/wiki/1920_Summer_Olympics "1920 Summer Olympics") in [Antwerp](/wiki/Antwerp "Antwerp"). A good run for Spain increased the popularity of football, and as a result, many teams gained professional status, and a league competition was established and scheduled for the [1928–29 season](/wiki/1929_La_Liga "1929 La Liga").

### 1928–1947: Segunda División and the top flight

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Deportivo_de_La_Coru%C3%B1a&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: 1928–1947: Segunda División and the top flight")]

In 1928, Dépor failed to qualify for the first ever [Primera División](/wiki/La_Liga "La Liga"), instead competing in the [Segunda División](/wiki/Segunda_Divisi%C3%B3n "Segunda División"), where it finished eighth out of ten. The team kept battling over the next few years in a division that was soon to undergo many structural and geographical changes. In 1932, in the Cup, Dépor defeated [Real Madrid](/wiki/Real_Madrid_CF "Real Madrid CF"), which had gone unbeaten through the entire season in the league.

In 1936, the [Spanish Civil War](/wiki/Spanish_Civil_War "Spanish Civil War") broke out, forcing the abandonment of all official competitions until the [1939–40 season](/wiki/1939%E2%80%9340_La_Liga "1939–40 La Liga"). That year, *The Herculeans* qualified for the promotion stage. The final game was against archrivals [Celta de Vigo](/wiki/Celta_de_Vigo "Celta de Vigo"), who were fighting to avoid relegation. Celta won 1–0 and remained in the top flight as Depor's hopes of promotion were denied. The following season, however, the club reached the promotion play-offs again, this time beating [Murcia](/wiki/Real_Murcia "Real Murcia") 2–1 to earn promotion to La Liga for the first time in the team's history.

The [first season](/wiki/1941%E2%80%9342_La_Liga "1941–42 La Liga") in the top flight saw the club finish fourth. However, the club declined in the next few seasons, finishing ninth, 12th and, in [1944–45](/wiki/1944%E2%80%9345_La_Liga "1944–45 La Liga"), 14th, resulting in relegation. Instant promotion was achieved the following year, but Dépor were relegated again at the end of the [1946–47 season](/wiki/1946%E2%80%9347_La_Liga "1946–47 La Liga"). Nonetheless, the team gained instant promotion in [1947–48](/wiki/1947%E2%80%9348_La_Liga "1947–48 La Liga").

This decade saw Depor's entrance to the top flight, so the club decided to build a new ground, [Riazor](/wiki/Estadio_Riazor "Estadio Riazor"), which remains their home today. It was opened on 28 October 1944 with a league game against [Valencia](/wiki/Valencia_CF "Valencia CF"). In this era, the key player of the team was [Juan Acuña](/wiki/Juan_Acu%C3%B1a "Juan Acuña"), the club's goalkeeper. "Xanetas", as he was known by locals, earned four [Zamora Trophies](/wiki/Ricardo_Zamora_Trophy "Ricardo Zamora Trophy") between 1942 and 1951, making him the second-most decorated keeper in the Spanish league.

### 1948–1957: The "Golden Decade"

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Deportivo_de_La_Coru%C3%B1a&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: 1948–1957: The \"Golden Decade\"")]

The club ended the [1948–49 season](/wiki/1948%E2%80%9349_La_Liga "1948–49 La Liga") in tenth place. The next season would see their first major achievement in the league; Dépor finished as runners-up, just one point behind [Atlético Madrid](/wiki/Atl%C3%A9tico_Madrid "Atlético Madrid") under the management of Argentinian [Alejandro Scopelli](/wiki/Alejandro_Scopelli "Alejandro Scopelli"), who brought to the club a group of South American players such as Julio Corcuera, Oswaldo García, Rafael Franco and [Dagoberto Moll](/wiki/Dagoberto_Moll "Dagoberto Moll"), a group that made the team more competitive and able to remain top flight status for nine consecutive seasons until [1957](/wiki/1956%E2%80%9357_La_Liga "1956–57 La Liga"). During that period, managers like [Helenio Herrera](/wiki/Helenio_Herrera "Helenio Herrera") and renowned players like [Pahiño](/wiki/Pahi%C3%B1o "Pahiño") and [Luis Suárez](/wiki/Luis_Su%C3%A1rez_(footballer,_born_1935) "Luis Suárez (footballer, born 1935)") played at the Riazor.

### 1958–1973: "Elevator Team"

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Deportivo_de_La_Coru%C3%B1a&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: 1958–1973: \"Elevator Team\"")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a2/Amancio_Amaro_1971.jpg/250px-Amancio_Amaro_1971.jpg)](/wiki/File:Amancio_Amaro_1971.jpg)

[Amancio Amaro](/wiki/Amancio_(footballer) "Amancio (footballer)") played for Deportivo between 1958 and 1962.

After nine seasons of first-tier football in Spain, the team was relegated to the Segunda División in 1957 and stayed there for five seasons until 1962, when they were promoted into the top flight. This started a yo-yo era that led the club to be known as the "elevator team"; promotions were achieved in 1962, 1964, 1966, 1968 and 1971, yet subsequent relegations occurred during 1963, 1965, 1967, 1970 and 1973.

Dépor had one of the best youth systems at the time in Spain, as youngsters including [Amancio Amaro](/wiki/Amancio_Amaro "Amancio Amaro"), [Severino Reija](/wiki/Severino_Reija "Severino Reija"), [José Luis Veloso](/wiki/Jos%C3%A9_Luis_Veloso "José Luis Veloso") and [Jaime Blanco](/wiki/Jaime_Blanco_Garc%C3%ADa "Jaime Blanco García") began their careers at the Riazor, in the future to become regular fixtures on the Spanish national team. Still more, including Luis Suárez, went on to play for larger clubs in Spain and Europe. However, the difficult financial situation of the club led to the forced sales of these players, and the team could not consolidate themselves in the top flight. This "yo-yo era" ended with relegation from the Primera División in [1973](/wiki/1972%E2%80%9373_La_Liga "1972–73 La Liga").

### 1974–1988: Dark times

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Deportivo_de_La_Coru%C3%B1a&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: 1974–1988: Dark times")]

After being relegated in 1973, the team struggled in the Second Division, failing to avoid another relegation and falling to the third tier ([Tercera División](/wiki/Tercera_Divisi%C3%B3n "Tercera División")) for the first time in their history. However, Dépor gained instant promotion and established themselves in the second tier for the rest of the decade. In [1980](/wiki/1979%E2%80%9380_Segunda_Divisi%C3%B3n "1979–80 Segunda División"), Dépor were again relegated to the newly created [Segunda División B](/wiki/Segunda_Divisi%C3%B3n_B "Segunda División B"), the third tier, again for the second time in their history. Again, however, the drop was short-lived as the team gained promotion the following season. Notably, Dépor were relegated and promoted along with arch-rivals Celta de Vigo, who played during the 1980–81 season the most attended games ever in Spain's third tier.

The club continued to play in the Second Division, showing little chance of being promoted back to the top flight. In the [1987–88](/wiki/1987%E2%80%9388_Segunda_Divisi%C3%B3n "1987–88 Segunda División") season, Dépor struggled and only avoided relegation after Vicente Celeiro scored a goal during added time against [Racing Santander](/wiki/Racing_de_Santander "Racing de Santander") in the last game of the season. This is often regarded as the end of the club's dark times and the beginning of a new era.

During this period, the club was heavily affected by financial difficulties and internal troubles with managers being sacked almost every year. In the summer of 1988, an open and popular club assembly chose a new board of directors headed by Augusto César Lendoiro. Deportivo had a debt estimated at 600 million [pesetas](/wiki/Spanish_peseta "Spanish peseta"), had been out of the top flight for 15 years and lacked established structures at economic and sporting level.

### 1989–1998: Resurgence and "Súper Dépor"

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Deportivo_de_La_Coru%C3%B1a&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: 1989–1998: Resurgence and \"Súper Dépor\"")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/85/Bebeto_cropped.jpg/250px-Bebeto_cropped.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bebeto_cropped.jpg)

[Bebeto](/wiki/Bebeto "Bebeto") was the symbol of Súper Dépor

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ea/Maurosilva.png/250px-Maurosilva.png)](/wiki/File:Maurosilva.png)

[Mauro Silva](/wiki/Mauro_Silva "Mauro Silva")

[Arsenio Iglesias](/wiki/Arsenio_Iglesias "Arsenio Iglesias"), a former Deportivo player and manager, was again appointed as manager in the 1988–89 season. Dépor had a lengthy run in the [Copa del Rey](/wiki/1988%E2%80%9389_Copa_del_Rey "1988–89 Copa del Rey"), though ultimately falling in the semi-finals to [Real Valladolid](/wiki/Real_Valladolid "Real Valladolid"). The next year, the team competed well in the league and qualified for the promotion play-offs, but expectations were denied again, this time by [Tenerife](/wiki/CD_Tenerife "CD Tenerife"). The [1990–91](/wiki/1990%E2%80%9391_Segunda_Divisi%C3%B3n "1990–91 Segunda División") season saw Dépor finishing as runners-up, finally achieving promotion to La Liga after an 18-year absence. Additionally, the club's finances began to improve and social support increased, especially amongst youth groups.

The [1991–92 season](/wiki/1991%E2%80%9392_Deportivo_de_La_Coru%C3%B1a_season "1991–92 Deportivo de La Coruña season"), the team's first back in the top flight, saw Dépor struggle, and they were forced to take part in the relegation play-off, beating [Real Betis](/wiki/Real_Betis "Real Betis") in a two-legged round. In 1992, Arsenio Iglesias began his fourth spell as Dépor manager; experienced players were also added to the team during that period, including [López Rekarte](/wiki/Luis_L%C3%B3pez_Rekarte "Luis López Rekarte"), [Paco Liaño](/wiki/Paco_Lia%C3%B1o "Paco Liaño"), [Claudio Barragán](/wiki/Claudio_Barrag%C3%A1n "Claudio Barragán"), [José Luis Ribera](/wiki/Jos%C3%A9_Luis_Ribera "José Luis Ribera"), [Adolfo Aldana](/wiki/Adolfo_Aldana "Adolfo Aldana") and [Donato](/wiki/Donato_(footballer) "Donato (footballer)"), along with promising youngsters such as local player [Fran](/wiki/Fran_(footballer,_born_1969) "Fran (footballer, born 1969)") and Brazilians [Bebeto](/wiki/Bebeto "Bebeto") and [Mauro Silva](/wiki/Mauro_Silva "Mauro Silva").

The [1992–93](/wiki/1992%E2%80%9393_Deportivo_de_La_Coru%C3%B1a_season "1992–93 Deportivo de La Coruña season") Deportivo squad experienced a resurgent season, placing in top positions all season and ultimately finishing third after champions [Barcelona](/wiki/FC_Barcelona "FC Barcelona") and runners-up Real Madrid, respectively, thus qualifying for European competition for the first time in their history. That year, the club also provided La Liga's [Pichichi Trophy](/wiki/Pichichi_Trophy "Pichichi Trophy") winner, Bebeto, and its Zamora Trophy winner, Paco Liaño. Additionally, Dépor made a historic comeback against Real Madrid at Riazor, trailing 2–0 at half-time but winning the game 3–2 and starting a run of 18 seasons to *Los Blancos* without a win at A Coruña.

In [1993–94](/wiki/1993%E2%80%9394_Deportivo_de_La_Coru%C3%B1a_season "1993–94 Deportivo de La Coruña season"), Dépor had another fantastic season, leading the table for the majority of the year and coming to the last matchday in first to face mid-table Valencia, knowing a win would ensure the first league title in club history. The game was very close, but near its end, Valencia's Serer conceded a penalty on [Nando](/wiki/Fernando_Mart%C3%ADnez_Perales "Fernando Martínez Perales"). Regular penalty-taker Donato had been substituted, so Serbian defender [Miroslav Đukić](/wiki/Miroslav_%C4%90uki%C4%87 "Miroslav Đukić") took it, but Valencia keeper González caught the ball; Deportivo saw their possible league title denied. After the frustration passed, the fans nonetheless recognised the impressive season the club had: Paco Liaño earned his second Zamora Trophy after conceding just 18 goals in 38 games, and Dépor made its debut in European competitions with the [UEFA Cup](/wiki/1993%E2%80%9394_UEFA_Cup "1993–94 UEFA Cup"), beating [Aalborg](/wiki/AaB_Fodbold "AaB Fodbold") and [Aston Villa](/wiki/Aston_Villa_F.C. "Aston Villa F.C.") but losing to [Eintracht Frankfurt](/wiki/Eintracht_Frankfurt "Eintracht Frankfurt") in the round of 16.

The [1994–95 season](/wiki/1994%E2%80%9395_Deportivo_de_La_Coru%C3%B1a_season "1994–95 Deportivo de La Coruña season") began with manager Arsenio Iglesias stating he would leave the club after the end of the year, though Dépor made another great campaign finishing again as runners-up, this time to Real Madrid. In that season's [UEFA Cup](/wiki/1994%E2%80%9395_UEFA_Cup "1994–95 UEFA Cup"), Deportivo were beaten again in the round of 16 by a German club, this time by [Borussia Dortmund](/wiki/Borussia_Dortmund "Borussia Dortmund") 3–2 on aggregate. Depor had won the first leg at home 1–0, and Dortmund equalized in the second leg to send the match into extra time. Now in extra time, Depor scored a second goal to put the match at 2–1, but with five minutes remaining, Dortmund scored two goals in two minutes to win the series. Despite this tough defeat, the season still hid a great surprise for the club, as Dépor made a fantastic run in [Copa del Rey](/wiki/1994%E2%80%9395_Copa_del_Rey "1994–95 Copa del Rey") and reached [the final](/wiki/1995_Copa_del_Rey_Final "1995 Copa del Rey Final") for the first time in club history, against Valencia. On 24 June 1995, at the [Santiago Bernabéu](/wiki/Santiago_Bernab%C3%A9u_Stadium "Santiago Bernabéu Stadium"), the final was levelled at 1–1 when referee García-Aranda suspended the game in the 80th minute due to heavy rain and a hailstorm. It was decided that the game would resume three days later. Two minutes after the match resumed, [Alfredo Santaelena](/wiki/Alfredo_Santaelena "Alfredo Santaelena") scored a header, which proved to be the winning goal that handed Depor their first major title (notwithstanding the 1912 [Concurso España](/wiki/Concurso_Espa%C3%B1a "Concurso España")).

The [1995–96](/wiki/1995%E2%80%9396_Deportivo_de_La_Coru%C3%B1a_season "1995–96 Deportivo de La Coruña season") season was an average one for Depor; they finished ninth in the league table, but reached the semi-finals of the [Cup Winners' Cup](/wiki/1995%E2%80%9396_UEFA_Cup_Winners%27_Cup "1995–96 UEFA Cup Winners' Cup"), losing to eventual champions [PSG](/wiki/Paris_Saint-Germain_F.C. "Paris Saint-Germain F.C."). The [1997–98](/wiki/1997%E2%80%9398_Deportivo_de_La_Coru%C3%B1a_season "1997–98 Deportivo de La Coruña season") season was very disappointing; the team only finished twelfth in the [league table](/wiki/1997%E2%80%9398_La_Liga "1997–98 La Liga"), failed to qualify for European competitions, were eliminated in the first round of the [UEFA Cup](/wiki/1997%E2%80%9398_UEFA_Cup "1997–98 UEFA Cup") by French club [Auxerre](/wiki/AJ_Auxerre "AJ Auxerre"), and were eliminated by second-tier side Deportivo Alaves in the [Copa del Rey](/wiki/1997%E2%80%9398_Copa_del_Rey "1997–98 Copa del Rey"). [José Manuel Corral](/wiki/Jos%C3%A9_Manuel_Corral_(footballer) "José Manuel Corral (footballer)") was replaced in the offseason by [Javier Irureta](/wiki/Javier_Irureta "Javier Irureta"). Under Irureta, the [1998–99 season](/wiki/1998%E2%80%9399_Deportivo_de_La_Coru%C3%B1a_season "1998–99 Deportivo de La Coruña season") was better and Deportivo finished sixth in the league, qualifying for the following season's UEFA Cup first round.

### 1999–2006: La Liga title, "El Centenariazo", and European Glory days

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Deportivo_de_La_Coru%C3%B1a&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: 1999–2006: La Liga title, \"El Centenariazo\", and European Glory days")]

In [1999–2000](/wiki/1999%E2%80%932000_La_Liga "1999–2000 La Liga"), Deportivo, managed by Irureta and with players like [Noureddine Naybet](/wiki/Noureddine_Naybet "Noureddine Naybet"), [Djalminha](/wiki/Djalminha "Djalminha"), [Fran](/wiki/Fran_(footballer,_born_1969) "Fran (footballer, born 1969)"), [Roy Makaay](/wiki/Roy_Makaay "Roy Makaay") and [Mauro Silva](/wiki/Mauro_Silva "Mauro Silva"), finally won their first La Liga title, five points ahead of Barcelona and Valencia.[[9]](#cite_note-9)[[10]](#cite_note-10) With this title, La Coruña became the second-smallest Spanish city with a population of approximately 250,000, behind [San Sebastián](/wiki/San_Sebasti%C3%A1n "San Sebastián") (home of [Real Sociedad](/wiki/Real_Sociedad "Real Sociedad")), to have ever won La Liga.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] In the UEFA Cup, the club reached the quarter-finals, being eliminated by eventual finalists [Arsenal](/wiki/Arsenal_F.C. "Arsenal F.C.").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/60/Diego_tristan.jpg/250px-Diego_tristan.jpg)](/wiki/File:Diego_tristan.jpg)

[Diego Tristán](/wiki/Diego_Trist%C3%A1n "Diego Tristán")

As league champions, Deportivo qualified for the [UEFA Champions League](/wiki/2000%E2%80%9301_UEFA_Champions_League "2000–01 UEFA Champions League") for the first time in its history. Their first match was a 1–1 draw against [Panathinaikos](/wiki/Panathinaikos_F.C. "Panathinaikos F.C.") in [Athens](/wiki/Athens "Athens"). They went on to top their group unbeaten with two wins and four draws. In the second group stage, they also topped their group, and Deportivo were now looking to be one of the strongest teams in the competition. However, in the quarter finals, they lost the first leg 3–0 to Leeds United, and although Depor won the second leg at Riazor 2–0, it was not enough and they were eliminated. The [following campaign](/wiki/2001%E2%80%9302_UEFA_Champions_League "2001–02 UEFA Champions League"), they were unbeaten in the first group stage again, including two wins against [Manchester United](/wiki/Manchester_United_F.C. "Manchester United F.C."). They progressed out of the second group stage into the quarter-finals, where they were eliminated by the team they beat twice in the group stage, Manchester United. On 8 September 2001, Deportivo played its 1,000th game in La Liga.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/dc/Real_Club_Deportivo_de_La_Coru%C3%B1a_league_performance_1929-2023.svg/250px-Real_Club_Deportivo_de_La_Coru%C3%B1a_league_performance_1929-2023.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Real_Club_Deportivo_de_La_Coru%C3%B1a_league_performance_1929-2023.svg)

Chart of Deportivo league performance 1929–2023

In the [2001–02 season](/wiki/2001%E2%80%9302_Deportivo_de_La_Coru%C3%B1a_season "2001–02 Deportivo de La Coruña season"), Depor finished league runner-up to Valencia and won the [Copa del Rey](/wiki/2001%E2%80%9302_Copa_del_Rey "2001–02 Copa del Rey") for a second time with a 2–1 win against Real Madrid on 6 March 2002. This match is commonly known in Spain as the *[Centenariazo](/wiki/Centenariazo "Centenariazo")*.[[11]](#cite_note-Centenariazo-11) Deportivo's opponents were expected to win the final comfortably as they were nicknamed the *[Galácticos](/wiki/Gal%C3%A1cticos "Galácticos")* and were among the strongest teams in Europe at the time. Additionally, the final was being played at their home ground, the Santiago Bernabéu. Everything was prepared so that after the expected victory, Real Madrid could celebrate their 100th anniversary by lifting the trophy in front of their own fans.[[11]](#cite_note-Centenariazo-11) However, Deportivo spoiled the party with a 2–1 win with goals from [Sergio](/wiki/Sergio_(footballer,_born_1976) "Sergio (footballer, born 1976)") and [Diego Tristán](/wiki/Diego_Trist%C3%A1n "Diego Tristán").[[12]](#cite_note-12)

Depor's best Champions League campaign was in the [2003–04 season](/wiki/2003%E2%80%9304_UEFA_Champions_League "2003–04 UEFA Champions League"), where they lost to eventual winners [Porto](/wiki/FC_Porto "FC Porto") by a narrow 1–0 aggregate scoreline in the semi-finals.[[13]](#cite_note-13) Although this was their best Champions League campaign, the club also had its biggest defeat in European competitions, losing 8–3 to [Monaco](/wiki/AS_Monaco_FC "AS Monaco FC") in the group stage.[[14]](#cite_note-14) However, this campaign is also remembered for their stunning comeback against [Milan](/wiki/A.C._Milan "A.C. Milan") in the quarter-finals. Milan won the first leg 4–1 at [San Siro](/wiki/San_Siro "San Siro"), but in the second leg at Riazor, Deportivo won 4–0 and eliminated the defending champions 5–4 on aggregate.[[15]](#cite_note-15)[[16]](#cite_note-16)

Deportivo had a mediocre [2004–05 season](/wiki/2004%E2%80%9305_Deportivo_de_La_Coru%C3%B1a_season "2004–05 Deportivo de La Coruña season"). The team finished eighth in [La Liga](/wiki/2004%E2%80%9305_La_Liga "2004–05 La Liga"), only good enough to qualify for the [Intertoto Cup](/wiki/UEFA_Intertoto_Cup "UEFA Intertoto Cup"). In the Champions League, the club had their worst campaign ever, finishing bottom of their group without winning a single match, and without scoring a single goal. This season ended the club's spell of five consecutive years playing Champions League football.

In the summer of 2005, manager [Javier Irureta](/wiki/Javier_Irureta "Javier Irureta") was replaced by [Joaquín Caparrós](/wiki/Joaqu%C3%ADn_Caparr%C3%B3s "Joaquín Caparrós"), ending his seven-year spell at the club.[[17]](#cite_note-17)

### 2007–2015: Transition and decline

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Deportivo_de_La_Coru%C3%B1a&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: 2007–2015: Transition and decline")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/16/Alineacion_Deportivo_-_CSKA_de_Mosc%C3%BA.jpg/250px-Alineacion_Deportivo_-_CSKA_de_Mosc%C3%BA.jpg)](/wiki/File:Alineacion_Deportivo_-_CSKA_de_Mosc%C3%BA.jpg)

Deportivo played in UEFA Cup in the 2008–09 season.

In the [2006–07 season](/wiki/2006%E2%80%9307_Deportivo_de_La_Coru%C3%B1a_season "2006–07 Deportivo de La Coruña season"), Deportivo finished 13th in the league, their worst finish since 1992, and Caparros resigned at the end of the season.[[18]](#cite_note-18) The [2007–08 season](/wiki/2007%E2%80%9308_Deportivo_de_La_Coru%C3%B1a_season "2007–08 Deportivo de La Coruña season") was a slight improvement, with the club finishing in 9th under new manager [Miguel Ángel Lotina](/wiki/Miguel_%C3%81ngel_Lotina "Miguel Ángel Lotina"), but were eliminated in the Copa del Rey by Espanyol at the first hurdle. Deportivo's [2008–09 season](/wiki/2008%E2%80%9309_Deportivo_de_La_Coru%C3%B1a_season "2008–09 Deportivo de La Coruña season") was better, with the club beating [Bnei Sakhnin](/wiki/Bnei_Sakhnin_F.C. "Bnei Sakhnin F.C.") in the [2008 Intertoto Cup](/wiki/2008_Intertoto_Cup "2008 Intertoto Cup") to progress to the UEFA Cup, where the club was eliminated 6–1 by Danish club [AaB](/wiki/AaB_Fodbold "AaB Fodbold") in the round of 32. Deportivo's period in the top flight came to an end as they were relegated after finishing 18th in [2010–11](/wiki/2010%E2%80%9311_La_Liga "2010–11 La Liga"). In July 2015, however, it was suspected that in the final round of matches for that season, [Levante](/wiki/UD_Levante "UD Levante") and [Real Zaragoza](/wiki/Real_Zaragoza "Real Zaragoza") were involved in a match-fixing scandal which ensured that the latter won 2–1 at the [Estadi Ciutat de València](/wiki/Estadi_Ciutat_de_Val%C3%A8ncia "Estadi Ciutat de València") and remained in the division at the expense of Deportivo, who lost 2–0 at home to Valencia.[[19]](#cite_note-:1-19) The case was in Courts until December 2020, when the match fixing was discarded by the [Provincial Court](/wiki/Audiencias_Provinciales_of_Spain "Audiencias Provinciales of Spain").[[19]](#cite_note-:1-19)

In the [2011–12 season](/wiki/2011%E2%80%9312_Segunda_Divisi%C3%B3n "2011–12 Segunda División"), Deportivo made an immediate return to the top flight, winning the title with a Segunda Division record of 91 points. [Lassad Nouioui](/wiki/Lassad_Nouioui "Lassad Nouioui") was the top scorer with 14 goals, [Andrés Guardado](/wiki/Andr%C3%A9s_Guardado "Andrés Guardado") the top assistant and [Álex Bergantiños](/wiki/%C3%81lex_Berganti%C3%B1os "Álex Bergantiños") the only player to participate in all league matches.

In the [2012–13 season](/wiki/2012%E2%80%9313_La_Liga "2012–13 La Liga"), Deportivo finished 19th after a turbulent campaign under three managers, and once again were relegated to the Segunda División. Deportivo, however, finished second in the [2013–14 season](/wiki/2013%E2%80%9314_Segunda_Divisi%C3%B3n "2013–14 Segunda División"), guaranteeing promotion to the top-flight for the second time in three years. A [2014–15 campaign](/wiki/2014%E2%80%9315_La_Liga "2014–15 La Liga") back in the top division with a disjointed squad featured some poor performances on the pitch under new manager [Víctor Fernández](/wiki/V%C3%ADctor_Fern%C3%A1ndez "Víctor Fernández"), including an 8–2 defeat at home to Real Madrid.[[20]](#cite_note-20) This was especially disappointing considering the 18-match unbeaten home run between 1992–93 and 2010–11 at the Riazor against Real Madrid, a feat which no other team has managed to achieve in the history of Spanish football.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Deportivo ultimately finished the season in 16th place and avoided relegation after appointing former player [Víctor Sánchez](/wiki/V%C3%ADctor_S%C3%A1nchez_(footballer,_born_1976) "Víctor Sánchez (footballer, born 1976)") as manager on 9 April 2015, for the remaining eight matches of the season.

### 2015–present: "New Dépor" and relegation to third division

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Deportivo_de_La_Coru%C3%B1a&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: 2015–present: \"New Dépor\" and relegation to third division")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/af/Deportivorealsociedad2.JPG/250px-Deportivorealsociedad2.JPG)](/wiki/File:Deportivorealsociedad2.JPG)

Match at [Riazor](/wiki/Estadio_Riazor "Estadio Riazor") in 2015

With Víctor Sánchez as their new manager, Deportivo made changes for the [new season](/wiki/2015%E2%80%9316_Deportivo_de_La_Coru%C3%B1a_season "2015–16 Deportivo de La Coruña season"). With the addition of new players [Alejandro Arribas](/wiki/Alejandro_Arribas "Alejandro Arribas"), [Fernando Navarro](/wiki/Fernando_Navarro_(Spanish_footballer) "Fernando Navarro (Spanish footballer)"), [Pedro Mosquera](/wiki/Pedro_Mosquera "Pedro Mosquera") and [Fayçal Fajr](/wiki/Fay%C3%A7al_Fajr "Fayçal Fajr"), Deportivo began the season with a 0–0 draw against Real Sociedad. On 28 November 2015, with a 2–0 victory against [Las Palmas](/wiki/UD_Las_Palmas "UD Las Palmas"), Deportivo were in fifth place after an impressive start. However, after a disastrous second half of the season, which was precipitated by a 3–0 home defeat to [Mirandés](/wiki/CD_Mirand%C3%A9s "CD Mirandés") in the [Copa del Rey](/wiki/2015%E2%80%9316_Copa_del_Rey "2015–16 Copa del Rey"), Deportivo won only two matches out of the next 22, including an 8–0 humiliation at the hands of Barcelona, and only secured their safety in La Liga in the penultimate game of the season with a victory over [Villarreal](/wiki/Villarreal_CF "Villarreal CF"). Victor Sánchez was sacked on 29 May 2016 after several incidents of player unrest within the squad.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The team continued in a negative spiral in the following seasons. At the end of the [2017–18 season](/wiki/2017%E2%80%9318_Deportivo_La_Coru%C3%B1a_season "2017–18 Deportivo La Coruña season"), Deportivo was relegated following a 4–2 home defeat to Barcelona, which also secured Barcelona the [La Liga](/wiki/2017%E2%80%9318_La_Liga "2017–18 La Liga") title.[[21]](#cite_note-21) The following season, the club played in Segunda División and finished sixth.[[22]](#cite_note-22) In the promotion play-offs, Deportivo lost to [Mallorca](/wiki/RCD_Mallorca "RCD Mallorca") 3–2 on aggregate and remained in Segunda División.[[23]](#cite_note-23) In the following campaign Deportivo was relegated once again, falling down into [Segunda División B](/wiki/Segunda_Divisi%C3%B3n_B "Segunda División B") for the first time in 39 years.[[24]](#cite_note-24) In 2020–21, the league system in Spain was reorganized and Deportivo was almost relegated to the newly formed fourth division, [Segunda Federación](/wiki/Segunda_Federaci%C3%B3n "Segunda Federación"), escaping relegation by just one point.[[25]](#cite_note-25) Thus, they remained in the third division, in the newly formed [Primera División RFEF](/wiki/Primera_Divisi%C3%B3n_RFEF "Primera División RFEF"), for the 2021–22 season.

After spending four seasons in the third tier, Deportivo finally won promotion back to the second division and thus professional football by finishing in first place in Group 1 of the [2023–24 Primera Federación](/wiki/2023%E2%80%9324_Primera_Federaci%C3%B3n "2023–24 Primera Federación"). The deciding match against [Barcelona Atlètic](/wiki/FC_Barcelona_Atl%C3%A8tic "FC Barcelona Atlètic") in the 36th round was attended by 31,833 spectators, a new Primera Federación attendance record, as Deportivo won 1–0 thanks to a goal from [Lucas Pérez](/wiki/Lucas_P%C3%A9rez "Lucas Pérez").[[26]](#cite_note-26)[[27]](#cite_note-27)

## Identity

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Deportivo_de_La_Coru%C3%B1a&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Identity")]

### Crest

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Deportivo_de_La_Coru%C3%B1a&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Crest")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/18/Flag_of_Galicia_%28civil%29.svg/250px-Flag_of_Galicia_%28civil%29.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Flag_of_Galicia_(civil).svg)

A sky blue diagonal strip on Deportivo crest is based on the [Galician flag](/wiki/Galician_flag "Galician flag").

Deportivo's crest contains cues to predecessor Sala Calvet's crest, with a gentlemen's belt encircling the purple and white banner of the gymnasium. The banner itself features a sky blue diagonal strip which represents the maritime flag of A Coruña and the [Galician flag](/wiki/Galician_flag "Galician flag"). In addition, it features a crown which represents its royal patronage (granted in 1909 by [Alfonso XIII](/wiki/Alfonso_XIII "Alfonso XIII")). During the [Spanish Republic](/wiki/Second_Spanish_Republic "Second Spanish Republic"), the honorific *real* (royal) and the crown were removed from the club crest; this was to return under Francoist Spain.

### Kit

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Deportivo_de_La_Coru%C3%B1a&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Kit")]

Deportivo have always played in their famous blue and white stripes, but it was not until 1912 that the club made these colours official for matches. Deportivo continues to wear blue and white striped shirts with blue shorts and socks, yet their second and third kits change annually according to commercial interests. Their current shirt sponsors are local brewery [Hijos de Rivera](/wiki/Hijos_de_Rivera_Brewery "Hijos de Rivera Brewery"), with [Kappa](/wiki/Kappa_(brand) "Kappa (brand)") manufacturing the kits. In 2015, Deportivo and [Estrella Galicia](/wiki/Estrella_Galicia "Estrella Galicia") extended their sponsorship deal throughout the 2015–16 La Liga season with an option of a further year.[[28]](#cite_note-28)

### Supporters

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Deportivo_de_La_Coru%C3%B1a&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Supporters")]

Deportivo's supporters are known as *deportivistas.* According to a 2007 survey by the [Centro de Investigaciones Sociológicas](/wiki/Centro_de_Investigaciones_Sociol%C3%B3gicas "Centro de Investigaciones Sociológicas"), Deportivo was the ninth team in Spain in terms of number of fans, with a total of 2.2%.[[29]](#cite_note-29) Fans organise themselves into around 200 fan groups known as *peñas*, with the most well-known being the "Riazor Blues". The interests of the supporters are represented by the Federación de Peñas RC Deportivo.

In the 2022–23 season, despite playing in the third tier, the club had 25,001 season ticket holders.[[30]](#cite_note-30) In March 2023, a match against [Real Madrid Castilla](/wiki/Real_Madrid_Castilla "Real Madrid Castilla") attracted a total of 26,745 fans, the fifth largest attendance of any football match played in Spain that weekend, bettered by only four teams from the top flight.[[31]](#cite_note-31) However, in the promotion play-offs, Deportivo fans broke the record again, reaching a total of 28,828 for the match against [Castellón](/wiki/CD_Castell%C3%B3n "CD Castellón").[[32]](#cite_note-32) That match was the fourth most attended match in Spain that weekend.[[33]](#cite_note-33)

## Women's team

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Deportivo_de_La_Coru%C3%B1a&action=edit&section=38 "Edit section: Women's team")]

Main article: [Deportivo de La Coruña (women)](/wiki/Deportivo_de_La_Coru%C3%B1a_(women) "Deportivo de La Coruña (women)")

Real Club Deportivo de La Coruña Femenino is the women's football section of Deportivo which plays in [Primera Federación](/wiki/Primera_Federaci%C3%B3n_(women) "Primera Federación (women)"), the second tier of Spanish women's football.

### Honours

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Deportivo_de_La_Coru%C3%B1a&action=edit&section=39 "Edit section: Honours")]

* Women's Spanish Cup (unofficial) (2): 1981, 1982
* [Women's Spanish Cup](/wiki/Copa_de_la_Reina_de_F%C3%BAtbol "Copa de la Reina de Fútbol") (3): 1983, 1984, 1985[[37]](#cite_note-38)

## Reserve team

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Deportivo_de_La_Coru%C3%B1a&action=edit&section=40 "Edit section: Reserve team")]

Main article: [Deportivo Fabril](/wiki/Deportivo_Fabril "Deportivo Fabril")

Real Club Deportivo Fabril is the reserve team of Deportivo de La Coruña. Founded in 1914 as Fabril Sociedad Deportiva, it plays in [Segunda Federación](/wiki/Segunda_Federaci%C3%B3n "Segunda Federación"), the fourth tier of Spanish football. Its stadium is called Cidade Deportiva de Abegondo, with a capacity of 1,000 seats.

In 1993, the team was officially renamed Deportivo B, although most locals still called it "Fabril". In 2017, it was renamed back to Real Club Deportivo Fabril.
